narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, we know how much heart and determination it takes to pursue this path, and we're here to help you understand the automated decision on your application in a clear way. Your profile includes Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles of 2, a middle family income bracket, an undergraduate GPA of 2.50, an LSAT score of 25, and plans to attend full-time—compared to typical students who often have higher deciles around the 5th or 6th, undergraduate GPAs near 3.21, and LSAT scores averaging 36.7. The model predicted a 52% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to pass confidently. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking of 2, well below the average, signaling to the model a notable dip in later academic performance relative to peers. This outweighed other influences, like your middle family income, which actually nudged the prediction slightly toward passing, perhaps reflecting some stability there compared to lower brackets. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.50, also below average, provided a mild positive counterbalance toward passing, as did your Year 1 decile of 2, showing early consistency even if not top-tier. Meanwhile, your LSAT score of 25 worked against you by pushing moderately toward a failure prediction, standing out as lower than what most successful bar takers achieve. Being full-time, like over 90% of students, offered a small favorable nudge, aligning with the typical path to success. Overall, your situation compares to many applicants by having some strengths like full-time commitment, but the model was most swayed by lower-than-average later grades and test scores, which carried more weight than your background factors. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just how this tool weighed the data from past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your later academic record through additional coursework or post-bac programs, and gathering strong recommendation letters that highlight your resilience as a Black woman in this field. Many have turned similar profiles around; reach out to admissions for advice on reapplying stronger next cycle—we believe in your journey.
